text.title: AC-to-Assertion Mapping Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every story's AC-to-assertion mapping must satisfy. The discipline is the only thing that exposes AI-generated structural-only tests at PR review.

rule.statement: The PR description (or a linked ac-mapping.yaml) contains an explicit mapping: for every AC in the user story, list at least one test file path plus test name. ACs without a mapping line are blockers on merge.

rationale.source: ATDD / specification-by-example research; AI-generated-test PR audit reports

rule.rationale: Without an explicit mapping, "I tested it" is unfalsifiable. Reviewers cannot verify coverage from line count alone, and AI-generated tests achieve coverage targets without testing behavior. The mapping is the single artifact that makes coverage verifiable.

example.title: AC mapping in PR description
example.code:
## AC-to-test mapping
| AC | Test path | Test name | Asserted behavior | |----|-----------|-----------|-------------------| | AC-1: user can submit valid form | tests/form.spec.ts | submits valid form and persists | response 200 AND db row exists | | AC-2: invalid email rejected | tests/form.spec.ts | rejects bad email | response 400 AND error.code = INVALID_EMAIL | | AC-3: rate-limited at 5/min | tests/form.rate.spec.ts | rate-limits after 5 attempts | 6th call returns 429 AND retry-after header set |

rule.statement: The `asserted_behavior` field must describe what BEHAVIOR was verified, not what code path was exercised. Forbidden: "method was called", "no exception raised", "status 200". Allowed: "response body contains expected user_id", "db row created with correct attributes", "side effect X observable".
rule.rationale: Structural assertions are what AI test-generators produce by default. "method was called" passes whether the method does anything meaningful or not. Behavior assertions test that the system actually delivered the AC's promise.

example.title: Structural assertion
Behavior assertion

rule.statement: An AC may map to multiple tests (happy path, edge case, failure mode), and a single test may cover multiple ACs. What is NOT allowed: an AC with zero tests, OR a test that exists in the diff but is not mapped to any AC.
rule.rationale: Many-to-many mapping reflects real testing structure (one test exercises multiple behaviors; one behavior is verified across multiple tests). The rule disallows orphan ACs and orphan tests, which together produce 90% of the coverage gaps the discipline exists to catch.

rule.statement: The mapping is in the PR description from the moment the PR is opened (or pre-populated by `scripts/extract-acs.py`). It is not added retroactively after review comments demand it. CI rejects PR-marks-ready transitions when the mapping is empty or contains placeholder text.
rule.rationale: Mappings added after review pressure tend to be retrofit jobs that match test names to ACs superficially. Up-front mapping forces the engineer to think about AC coverage while writing the tests, which is the only time it costs nothing.

rule.statement: PR reviewer's mandatory checklist includes: read each AC, locate the listed test, read the asserted_behavior, and reject the PR if the assertion does not actually verify the AC's promise. Reviewer time on this step is ~5 minutes per story; tracked as a review-quality metric.
rule.rationale: Without an enforced reviewer step, the mapping becomes another field nobody reads. The 5-minute walk is the moment the discipline cashes in. Reviewer effectiveness on this step is the leading indicator of whether the practice is real or theatre.

rule.statement: Stop applying this methodology when none of the preconditions in `AGENTS.md` "Applies If" hold (no ACs in stories, no PR review, no real users) OR when a compliance system already maps every test to a formal requirement — in those cases the discipline duplicates an existing process.
rule.rationale: Forcing a mapping artefact where the upstream artefact (ACs) does not exist produces theatre. The skip leaf in the decision tree resolves to this rule when the input precondition fails.

list.item: State assertions (db row exists, file written, queue contains message)
Output assertions (response body shape and content, returned object fields)
Side-effect assertions (email sent, webhook invoked with payload X)
Negative assertions (this attempt failed with this specific error code)
NOT behavior: "method was called", "no exception", "status 200 alone", "code reached this line"
